Physical Properties.q: Why are Zn, Cd, and Hg not considered transition elements?.
Physical Properties.a: They have completely filled  $$d^{10}$$   configurations in their ground state and in their common oxidation states1.

Physical Properties.q: In the 3d series, which element has the lowest enthalpy of atomization and why?.
Physical Properties.a: Zinc (Zn). It has no unpaired electrons (configuration  $$3d^{10}4s^2$$  ), resulting in the weakest interatomic metallic bonding.

Physical Properties.q: Why is the melting point of Manganese (Mn) anomalously low compared to its neighbors?.
Physical Properties.a: While generally  $$d^5$$   implies strong bonding, Mn shows a dip due to its stable half-filled configuration forming a complex lattice with weaker metallic bonding.

Physical Properties.q: Why do the atomic radii of the second (4d) and third (5d) transition series exhibit very similar values (e.g., Zr â‰ˆ Hf)?.
Physical Properties.a: Due to Lanthanoid Contraction. The filling of 4f orbitals before 5d results in poor shielding, pulling 5d electrons in and cancelling the size increase.

Physical Properties.q: Why is the separation of Zirconium (4d) and Hafnium (5d) extremely difficult?
Physical Properties.a: Due to Lanthanoid Contraction, they have almost identical atomic radii and consequently very similar chemical properties.

Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.q: Why is the first Ionisation Enthalpy (IE) of Zn higher than expected, while Cr is lower?.
Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.a: Zn has a stable filled  $$4s^2$$  subshell. Cr has $$3d^5 4s^1$$  ; removing the 4s electron leaves the stable half-filled  $$d^5$$   intact.

Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.q: Why is the second Ionisation Enthalpy of Cu unusually high?.
Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.a: Removal of the second electron disrupts the stable completely filled d-shell ( $$3d^{10}$$ for $$Cu^+$$  ), causing loss of exchange energy.

Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.q: Identify the transition element in the 3d series that does not exhibit variable oxidation states.
Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.a: Scandium (Sc). It only exhibits +3 because losing all valence electrons ( $$3d^1 4s^2$$  ) yields a stable noble gas configuration.

Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.q: In Group 6, why is Cr(VI) a strong oxidizing agent while Mo(VI) and W(VI) are stable?.
Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.a: Heavier d-block members favor higher oxidation states. Thus, Mo(VI) and W(VI) are stable, while Cr(VI) is unstable and reduces easily.

Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.q: Why is the highest oxidation state of a transition metal usually exhibited in its oxides or fluorides?.
Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.a: Due to the small size and high electronegativity of O and F. Oxygen stabilizes high states via multiple bonds ( $$p\pi-d\pi$$  ).

Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.q: Which 3d metal fluoride exhibits the +7 oxidation state? (Trick Question).
Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.a: None. The highest fluoride is  $$MnF_4$$  . The +7 state is only achieved in oxides ( $$Mn_2O_7$$  ) or oxofluorides.

Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.q: Why is $$Cu^{2+}(aq) $$more stable than$$  Cu^+(aq) $$despite$$  Cu^+ $$having a stable$$  3d^{10}$$configuration?
Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.a: The high negative Hydration Enthalpy of $$Cu^{2+}$$   compensates for the energy required to remove the second electron (2nd IE).

Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.q: Why is the highest oxidation state of Mn +7 in oxides ( $$Mn_2O_7$$  ) but only +4 in fluorides ( $$MnF_4$$  )?
Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.a: Oxygen can form multiple bonds ( $$p\pi-d\pi$$  ) with the metal, stabilizing high oxidation states, whereas fluorine can only form single bonds.

Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.q: In Group 6, why is  $$Cr(VI)$$a strong oxidant (e.g., Dichromate) while$$Mo(VI)$$and$$W(VI)$$   are stable?
Ionisation Enthalpy & Oxidation States.a: In transition groups, the stability of higher oxidation states increases down the group (heavier elements are more stable in high OS).

Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).q: Why is the  $$E^\circ (M^{2+}/M)$$   value for Copper positive (+0.34 V)?.
Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).a: The high energy required to sublime and ionize Cu is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy, making it noble.

Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).q: Why are  $$E^\circ$$   values for Mn, Ni, and Zn more negative than the trend suggests?.
Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).a: Mn & Zn: Stability of half-filled ( $$d^5$$  ) and filled ( $$d^{10}$$  ) subshells. Ni: Exceptionally high negative hydration enthalpy.

Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).q: Why is  $$Cr^{2+}$$a reducing agent while$$Mn^{3+}$$is an oxidizing agent (both are$$d^4$$  )?.
Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).a: $$Cr^{2+}$$ reduces to stable $$d^3$$  ( $$t_{2g}^3$$  ). $$Mn^{3+}$$ oxidizes to stable half-filled $$d^5$$.

Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).q: Why is $$Co^{3+}$$a strong oxidizing agent in aqueous solution?.
Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).a: It accepts an electron to form stable $$Co^{2+}$$  . However, with strong ligands,  $$Co^{3+}$$   stabilizes due to crystal field splitting.

Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).q: Why is the $$E^\circ (M^{3+}/M^{2+})$$value for Scandium very low?.
Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).a: $$Sc^{3+}$$ has a stable noble gas configuration. $$Sc^{2+}$$   would instantly lose an electron to reach this state.

Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).q: Why does Copper not replace Hydrogen from acids ( $$E^\circ_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34V$$  )?
Standard Electrode Potentials (EÂ°).a: The energy required to sublime and ionize Cu (high  $$\Delta_a H$$   and IE) is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy.

Magnetic Properties & Colour.q: Calculate the 'spin-only' magnetic moment for a divalent ion ( $$Z=25$$  ).

Magnetic Properties & Colour.a: $$Ti^{4+}$$ is $$3d^0$$  (no d-d transition possible). $$Ti^{3+}$$ is $$3d^1$$   (absorbs visible light for d-d transition).

Magnetic Properties & Colour.q: Why does the magnetic moment of  $$Co^{2+}$$   differ from the spin-only value?.
Magnetic Properties & Colour.a: The spin-only formula ignores orbital angular momentum, which is not fully quenched in some ions.

Magnetic Properties & Colour.q: $$Cr^{3+} $$and$$  Co^{2+} $$both have 3 unpaired electrons. Why is the magnetic moment of$$  Co^{2+}$$higher?
Magnetic Properties & Colour.a: $$Co^{2+}$$ has a significant contribution from **orbital angular momentum**, whereas it is quenched in $$Cr^{3+}$$  .

Magnetic Properties & Colour.q: Which of these is colourless:  $$TiCl_3$$  or $$TiCl_4$$  ? Why?
Magnetic Properties & Colour.a: $$TiCl_4$$   is colourless because  $$Ti^{4+}$$   has a  $$d^0$$   configuration (no unpaired electrons for d-d transition).

Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.a: $$CN^-$$   is a strong field ligand (pairing e-, $$dsp^2$$  ).  $$Cl^-$$   is weak field (unpaired e-,  $$sp^3$$  ).

Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.q: Why do transition metals form many complexes?.
Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.a: 1. Small metal ion size. 2. High ionic charge. 3. Availability of d-orbitals for bonding.

Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.q: How do transition metals act as catalysts?.
Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.a: 1. Surface Adsorption: 3d/4s electrons bond with reactants, lowering activation energy. 2. Variable Oxidation States: Forming unstable intermediates.

Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.q: What physical property do interstitial compounds (like  $$TiC$$  ) retain from the metal?.
Why are interstitial compounds chemically inert and hard?.
Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.a: Small atoms (H, C, N) trapped in the lattice form bonds that rigidify the structure.

Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.q: Why are transition metal alloys readily formed?.
Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.a: Transition metals have very similar atomic radii (within ~15%), allowing easy atom exchange in the lattice.

Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.q: How does the chemical reactivity and conductivity of interstitial compounds compare to the parent metal?
Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.a: They are chemically inert (less reactive) but retain the metallic conductivity of the parent metal.

Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.q: In the reaction between Iodide and Persulphate catalysed by Fe(III), what role does Fe(II) play?
Complexes, Catalysts & Alloys.a: Fe(III) oxidises Iodide and becomes Fe(II). Fe(II) then reduces Persulphate and regenerates Fe(III).

Potassium Dichromate.q: What is the starting material for  $$K_2Cr_2O_7$$  prep?.
Potassium Dichromate.a: Chromite ore ( $$FeCr_2O_4$$  ). Fused with $$Na_2CO_3$$  + air to form $$Na_2CrO_4$$.

Potassium Dichromate.q: Why is  $$Na_2Cr_2O_7$$  NOT used as a primary standard?.
Potassium Dichromate.a: It is hygroscopic (absorbs moisture). $$K_2Cr_2O_7$$   is not.

Potassium Dichromate.q: What happens to pH and color if alkali is added to Dichromate?.
Potassium Dichromate.a: Solution turns Orange ( $$Cr_2O_7^{2-}$$  ) to Yellow ( $$CrO_4^{2-}$$  ). pH increases.

Potassium Dichromate.q: Write the ionic equation for $$Fe^{2+}$$oxidation by dichromate.
What is the Cr-O-Cr bond angle in  $$Cr_2O_7^{2-}$$  ?.
Potassium Dichromate.a: 126Â°. The structure is two tetrahedra sharing one oxygen.

Potassium Dichromate.q: Explain the colour change when an acidic solution of Dichromate (Orange) is made alkaline.
Potassium Dichromate.a: It turns Yellow due to the formation of Chromate ions ( $$CrO_4^{2-}$$  ).  $$Cr_2O_7^{2-} \xrightarrow{OH^-} CrO_4^{2-}$$  .

Potassium Permanganate.a: Due to Charge Transfer spectra (ligand-to-metal), not d-d transitions.

Potassium Permanganate.q: How is green manganate converted to permanganate in the lab?.
What happens when  $$KMnO_4$$  is heated at 513 K?.
Why avoid HCl in permanganate titrations?.
Reaction of Permanganate with Iodide (Acidic vs Neutral)?.
Why is HCl not used to acidify  $$KMnO_4$$  solutions in titrations?
Potassium Permanganate.a: $$KMnO_4$$   is a strong oxidizing agent and oxidises HCl to chlorine gas ( $$Cl_2$$  ), which interferes with the titration.

Potassium Permanganate.q: What happens when green Manganate ion ( $$MnO_4^{2-}$$  ) is placed in an acidic or neutral solution?
Potassium Permanganate.a: It disproportionates into Permanganate ( $$MnO_4^-$$  ) and Manganese Dioxide ( $$MnO_2$$  ).

The Lanthanoids (4f Series).q: Why is separation of Lanthanoids difficult?.
The Lanthanoids (4f Series).a: Lanthanoid Contraction makes their ionic radii and chemical properties almost identical.

The Lanthanoids (4f Series).q: Which Lanthanoid exhibits stable +4 state and why?.
The Lanthanoids (4f Series).a: Cerium (Ce). Losing 4 electrons ( $$4f^1 5d^1 6s^2$$  ) gives a stable noble gas config.
It has stable half-filled $$f^7$$  , but reverts to the common +3 state characteristic of the series.

The Lanthanoids (4f Series).q: Which trivalent Lanthanoid ions are diamagnetic?.

The Lanthanoids (4f Series).a: ~95% Lanthanoid metal (Ce, La, Nd), ~5% Iron, traces of S, C, Ca, Al.

The Lanthanoids (4f Series).q: What is the trend in the basic character of Lanthanoid hydroxides ( $$La(OH)_3$$  to $$Lu(OH)_3$$  ) and why?
The Lanthanoids (4f Series).a: Basic character decreases. Due to Lanthanoid contraction, cation size decreases, increasing the covalent character of the M-OH bond.

The Actinoids (5f Series).q: Why is Actinoid Contraction greater than Lanthanoid?.
The Actinoids (5f Series).a: 5f electrons provide poorer shielding of nuclear charge than 4f electrons.

The Actinoids (5f Series).q: Why do Actinoids show a wide range of oxidation states?.
The Actinoids (5f Series).a: The energies of 5f, 6d, and 7s subshells are comparable (very small gaps).

The Actinoids (5f Series).q: Which Actinoids show max oxidation state +7?.
Why are IE of early Actinoids lower than Lanthanoids?.
The Actinoids (5f Series).a: 5f orbitals penetrate less into the core, so electrons are less shielded and held loosely.
1. Radioactivity. 2. Wide range of OS. 3. Tendency to hydrolyze/form complexes.

The Actinoids (5f Series).q: Name the last Actinoid and its stable OS.
Reaction of Actinoids with boiling water?.
The Actinoids (5f Series).a: Forms a mixture of Oxide and Hydride.

The Actinoids (5f Series).q: Which actinoid formally has no 5f electrons in ground state?.
Why do Actinoids form complexes more readily and use f-orbitals in bonding compared to Lanthanoids?
The Actinoids (5f Series).a: 5f orbitals extend farther from the nucleus and are available for bonding, whereas 4f orbitals are deeply buried and shielded.

Chemical Properties of Transition Metals.q: What happens when $$Cu^{2+}$$is treated with Iodide ions ( $$I^-$$  )? (Write the observation).
Chemical Properties of Transition Metals.a: A white precipitate of $$Cu_2I_2$$  is formed, and Iodine ( $$I_2$$  ) is liberated ( $$Cu^{2+}$$   oxidises $$I^-$$  ).
